text: Hey everyone, I'm Shaw. In this video, I'm going to share how I taught Claude to edit my YouTube videos. I'll start with an overview of my approach for doing this, and then walk through how it works. Key part of my process for teaching Claude how to edit videos was using so-called agent skills. All these are are reusable instructions that give Claude competence. In other words, skills teach Claude how to do things well. All a skill is is a folder with some files and folders in it. The main thing in this skill folder is going to be this skill.md file. MD stands for markdown, and it's just a text file. This is basically a specialized prompt that Claude can read every time you want it to use this skill. But you can also have other things in the skill folder, so you can have different references, you can have assets if you wanted to run code and have scripts ready to go to do that, you can do that as well. And the main benefit of using skills is this idea of so-called progressive disclosure. This is just a fancy way of saying skills give Claude the right context at the right time. Anytime you open up a new session with Claude, whether that's on the desktop app or you're using Claude code in the terminal, the way Claude knows what skills it has available is that in this skill.md file, there's going to be a short description of what that skill is and when to use it. And that short description is going to be made available to Claude at the start of every new conversation. Then, if the skill sounds relevant, so if I ask Claude, "Please edit this YouTube video." and I have a skill called YouTube video editor, Claude will be smart enough to realize this skill is relevant, and then it'll load the entire skill.md file into the conversation. But then, if in the file, things like generating transcripts, doing audio normalization, referencing editing principles, and so on and so forth, it can grab that additional context as needed. We'll see a concrete example of this in the next slide with the video editor skill. But first, you might be wondering why use skills to teach Claude how to do things. So, the way I see it, there are three key benefits to using agent skills as opposed to trying to get Claude to do this task or any other task some other way, which is first, skills allow you to teach Claude how to do something once, and then it knows how to do that thing forever. So, maybe you've experienced this where there are certain things that you want Claude to do over and over again, like summarizing emails or writing blog posts, analyzing meeting transcripts, and on and on and on. And maybe every time you want Claude or ChatGPT or whatever model to do this, you have to re-explain the task and re-explain what you want it to do every single time. And this just becomes quite cumbersome. Another benefit of skills is that you can iteratively improve them and update them every single time you use them. Finally, skills can be mixed and matched. For example, you might have a skill for copywriting, you might have a skill that helps you with your admin work, you might have a skill that helps you with video editing, you might have a skill that knows how to navigate your Notion account, and on and on and on. And maybe you want to mix and match some of these things. So, for example, maybe you have some notes about a specific project sitting in your Notion account, but you want Claude to be able to grab those notes and then write an email that sounds like you, you can mix and match those two different skills. You can use your Notion navigation skill plus an admin skill plus an email writer skill, and Claude can mix and match those to get tasks done. So, instead of having one rigid prompt for every single workflow or every type of thing you do in your work, you can just have these general skills that can be combined to execute specific workflows on the fly. Basically, all it is is a text file that tells Final Cut Pro, which is a video editing software, how to present a raw video based on a set of edits. First thing it's going to do is generate a transcript. That's going to be using this analyze.py and this transcribe.py file, and it's using this service called AssemblyAI. This is actually really important point because I couldn't just use any transcription tool cuz a lot of transcription tools will clean up the transcript before presenting it to you. But the thing is, we don't want the transcript to be cleaned up. We want it to be full of all the ums, the uhs, all the filler words, so that when we give the transcript to Claude, it knows what parts of the video to cut out. So, we don't want it to be preprocessed, and AssemblyAI was one solution that I found that gives you the true transcript full of all the mistakes and so-called disfluencies. Then, after the transcript is generated, it's going to run through a set of rules or heuristics for generating an edit. This isn't Claude doing anything, this is just running some Python code. So, that's this proposed_edits.py file, but then, the proposed_edits.py is not going to be so great. So, if you've ever tried to automate video editing, you've probably realized how hard it is because it's not enough to use heuristics to cut up a video because a lot of decisions that you have to make during video editing are editorial decisions. Should I pick this take or this other take, or is this part of the video actually adding value? Could I just take it out and follow principle of less is more? And the principles you use will be dependent on the type of content that you're making or the personal style and preferences that you have for the videos that you make. So, this is actually pretty complicated process. However, by using Claude to refine the edits from this rule-based process, I was able to get something that's much more helpful than what you can just automate with code. Now, once Claude proposes some edits, there's some more code that's run that turns into this FCPXML file, and then I can just import this file into Final Cut Pro and just continue editing. Before, a typical video that I would post on YouTube would go through at least three cuts. The way I did this was using a four-step framework, which I talked about in my previous video, which looks like this. The first step is to do whatever it is I'm trying to do one time with Claude, then to distill that long conversation I have with Claude into a reusable skill, then I test the skill on a new example, and then I iterate and improve the skill over time. This is the framework I've been using to basically automate all parts of my work and onboard Claude onto my team. But specifically for this video editor, the way this worked was I started this whole process from scratch. I just gave Claude like a high-level description of what I wanted it to do, and then I tested the whole process using an initial 6-minute clip. I wanted it to be small, just so I could evaluate Claude's performance very quickly. And then I just went through a few rounds of this. So I'd give Claude feedback, it would update the 6-minute clip, I would look at the 6-minute clip again, give Claude more feedback, it would produce a new clip, I would review it in Final Cut Pro, give it more feedback, and I went through this loop about three or four times until it got to a place that it was pretty decent. This was probably like a 30-minute to a 60-minute conversation. So it's a very long conversation I was having with Claude.
